description:
You are a security log-analyst who has just received a short HTTP access log that the SOC
    (Security Operations Centre) generated during a test exercise.  
    Your job is to identify every *unique* hostname that appears in the log, try to resolve each
    of them to an IPv4 address, and then write a very small
    comma-separated report so the next analyst in the chain can ingest it automatically.

    1. Source log  
       • Path: /home/user/network/access.log  
       • Example lines (do **not** edit this file):  
         10.0.0.15 - - [12/Dec/2023:10:15:21 +0000] "GET http://localhost/index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 512  
         10.0.0.15 - - [12/Dec/2023:10:15:22 +0000] "GET http://example.com/assets/logo.png HTTP/1.1" 200 1024  
         10.0.0.15 - - [12/Dec/2023:10:15:25 +0000] "GET http://nonexistent.domain.tld/register HTTP/1.1" 404 123  

       The file is already present; just read from it.

    2. Hostname extraction rules  
       • Extract only the hostname part that appears between “http://” (or “https://”)  
         and the next “/”.  
       • Ignore duplicates; we only want one attempt per distinct hostname.

    3. Name-resolution rules  
       • Use any standard Linux CLI tool you like (host, dig, getent, etc.) to obtain
         the first IPv4 address for each hostname.  
       • If the hostname cannot be resolved to any IPv4 address, record exactly the string
         NO_RECORD (all capitals, no spaces).  
       • IPv6 addresses must be ignored, even if they appear in the resolver output.

    4. Output  
       • Create the output directory /home/user/analysis (create it if it does not exist).  
       • Write the file /home/user/analysis/dns_summary.csv with one line per hostname, in
         alphabetical order.  
       • Each line must be strictly of the form:  

           hostname,ipv4_or_NO_RECORD  

         with no leading or trailing whitespace.  
       • The file must contain exactly the following three lines once your work is complete
         (order is important because of the alphabetical requirement):

           example.com,93.184.216.34  
           localhost,127.0.0.1  
           nonexistent.domain.tld,NO_RECORD  

    5. Hints (optional reading, you may ignore):  
       • Typical command sequence is: “extract → sort/uniq → resolve → redirect”.  
       • Remember that CSV means “comma separated”, so no extra spaces around the comma.  
       • The grading script will read the file verbatim, so match
         the format precisely.

    A correct solution will leave /home/user/analysis/dns_summary.csv present with the three exact
    lines shown in step 4, and nothing else.  You do not need to print anything else to the screen
    beyond what your normal command usage outputs.
truth:
Initial state supplied by the test harness
    ------------------------------------------
    1. /home/user/network directory exists with permissions 0755.
    2. /home/user/network/access.log exists with permissions 0644 and the
       following exact contents (three lines, LF line endings):

       10.0.0.15 - - [12/Dec/2023:10:15:21 +0000] "GET http://localhost/index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 512
       10.0.0.15 - - [12/Dec/2023:10:15:22 +0000] "GET http://example.com/assets/logo.png HTTP/1.1" 200 1024
       10.0.0.15 - - [12/Dec/2023:10:15:25 +0000] "GET http://nonexistent.domain.tld/register HTTP/1.1" 404 123

    Expected state after agent finishes
    ------------------------------------
    • Directory /home/user/analysis exists with permissions 0755 (or more restrictive).
    • File /home/user/analysis/dns_summary.csv exists, permissions 0644, and **its entire
      byte-for-byte contents** are exactly:

      example.com,93.184.216.34
      localhost,127.0.0.1
      nonexistent.domain.tld,NO_RECORD

      (three lines, each terminated by a single LF, no trailing blank line).

    Anything else in /home/user/analysis will be ignored by tests; only the presence
    and exact contents of dns_summary.csv are graded.
